choice_rules.name: Part B Choice
choice_rules.feedback: Answer disregarded as student attempted more than two questions in Part B (only the first two attempts are graded).

concepts.description: Defining encapsulation as binding data and functions into a single unit (class).
Mentioning data hiding or controlling access via access specifiers (private/public).
Defining inheritance as a mechanism where a new class acquires properties of an existing class.
Mentioning code reusability or hierarchical relationships (base/derived class).
Defining a constructor as a special member function of a class that initializes objects.
Mentioning constructor properties (same name as class, no return type, called automatically on object creation).
Defining polymorphism as 'many forms' or the ability of a message/function to be processed in more than one way.
Mentioning the types of polymorphism (compile-time/static and run-time/dynamic).
Defining abstraction as hiding internal implementation details and showing only essential features to the user.
Mentioning how it is achieved (e.g. using classes, access specifiers, or header files).

questions.description: Explain Inheritance with a suitable example.

concepts.description: Defining inheritance, base/derived classes, and how child class inherits parent features.
Explaining the role of visibility/access specifiers (public, protected, private) in inheritance.
Providing a valid C++ code example showing base and derived classes.
Explaining how the example demonstrates code reuse (derived class accessing base class members).

questions.description: Explain the advantages of Encapsulation in Object-Oriented Programming.

concepts.description: Detailed explanation of data hiding/security (using private variables to restrict direct external access).
Explaining the control/validation provided by getter/setter methods (read-only access, constraints).
Explaining ease of code maintainability/modularity (changing internal details without breaking client code).

questions.description: Differentiate between Compile-Time Polymorphism and Run-Time Polymorphism with suitable examples.

concepts.description: Defining compile-time polymorphism, early/static binding, and overload resolution.
Defining run-time polymorphism, late/dynamic binding, and virtual function dispatch.
Comparing key differences (speed/performance, runtime overhead, compile-time detection).
Providing C++ code/example demonstrating function overloading or operator overloading.
Providing C++ code/example demonstrating virtual functions, base pointers, and overriding.
